steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Fleming's left-hand rule is used to find…
options: The direction of an induced current in a generator
The direction of the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field
The direction of the magnetic field around a solenoid
The magnitude of the force on a current-carrying conductor
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Fleming's left-hand rule gives the direction of the force (motor effect) — use your left hand for motors. Fleming's right-hand rule is used for generators (induced current). Neither rule gives you a magnitude.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! The left-hand rule tells you the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field — the motor effect.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.7.3 — Fleming's left-hand rule

steps.title: Fleming's Left-Hand Rule
steps.bodyHTML: Fleming's left-hand rule gives the direction of the force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field. Hold your left hand with your first finger pointing in the direction of the magnetic field (N to S), your second finger pointing in the direction of conventional current, and your thumb points in the direction of the force (motion). Remember: FBI — Force (thumb), B-field (first finger), current I (second finger).

relationships: First finger → magnetic Field (N to S)
Use LEFT hand for motor effect

commonErrors: Using the right hand instead of the left hand (right hand is for generators)
Pointing the first finger along the current instead of the field
Using electron flow direction instead of conventional current direction

aqa.questionText: A horizontal wire carries a conventional current flowing to the right. The magnetic field points vertically upwards. Using Fleming's left-hand rule, describe the direction of the force on the wire.

steps.label: Set up the left hand
steps.content: Point the first finger of the left hand upwards (direction of magnetic field B). Point the second finger to the right (direction of conventional current).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct identification of finger assignments

steps.label: Read off the thumb direction
steps.content: With first finger pointing up and second finger pointing right, the thumb points out of the page (towards you). This is the direction of the force on the wire.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct direction: out of the page

aqa.finalAnswer: The force acts out of the page (towards the observer).

aqa.questionText: When using Fleming's left-hand rule, which finger represents the direction of conventional current?
markSchemeSteps: Second finger → current (I); first finger → field (B); thumb → force (motion) ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Option B is a common swap — the first finger points along the magnetic field, not the current.
